title: Security Architecture & Secure Design
description: Master the Secure Development Lifecycle (SDLC), threat modeling with STRIDE, secure design principles from Saltzer & Schroeder, defence-in-depth architecture, and risk assessment — building security INTO systems from the ground up.
learning_outcomes: Explain the phases of the Secure Development Lifecycle (SDLC)
Apply STRIDE threat modeling to identify threats in a system
Implement Saltzer and Schroeder's secure design principles in code
Design defence-in-depth architectures with multiple security layers
Apply the principle of least privilege in system architecture
Implement fail-safe defaults in application code
Design systems with complete mediation (every access checked)
Apply separation of duties and economy of mechanism
Build security into every phase of development, not as an afterthought
Understand protection vs distribution trade-offs in architecture
Create a threat model document for a web application

ai_tutor_config.personality: Encouraging, patient, uses real-world analogies. Never judges. Celebrates small wins. Thinks of security architecture like building a castle — every wall, moat, and gate has a purpose!

intervention_triggers.idle_40_seconds: Security architecture is about thinking BEFORE coding. Imagine you are an architect designing a bank vault — every decision matters. Want me to walk through the key principles?
intervention_triggers.idle_90_seconds: Still thinking? That is great — architects think a LOT before they build! Let me simplify: secure design means building security INTO the system from day one, not bolting it on later. Which principle would you like to explore?
intervention_triggers.idle_180_seconds: No rush at all! Think of it this way: a castle has multiple defences — moat, walls, guards, locked doors. That is defence-in-depth. Each layer stops different attacks. Let us start with one layer and build up!
intervention_triggers.first_error: Good thinking! Architecture is about trade-offs — there is rarely one right answer. Let me show you the reasoning behind the best practice.
intervention_triggers.repeated_errors: These concepts are more abstract than coding, so let me give you a concrete analogy and a step-by-step approach.
intervention_triggers.after_success: Excellent! You are thinking like a security architect now. These decisions prevent entire categories of vulnerabilities!

encouragement_phrases: Security architects prevent vulnerabilities before a single line of code is written!
STRIDE threat modeling is used by Microsoft, Google, and every major tech company!
Defence-in-depth is why banks have vaults inside locked rooms inside guarded buildings!
The SDLC ensures security is considered at EVERY stage — not just at the end!
Saltzer and Schroeder's principles from 1975 are STILL the gold standard today!
Fail-safe defaults mean if something goes wrong, the system stays SECURE!
Complete mediation means EVERY access is checked — no shortcuts!
Economy of mechanism: simpler code = fewer bugs = fewer vulnerabilities!
You are learning to think like an attacker to build like a defender!
These architectural decisions protect millions of users!

explanation_styles.analogy: Building a secure system is like building a medieval castle. The SDLC is your construction plan. Threat modeling is scouting for enemy attack routes. Design principles are your architectural rules (thick walls, narrow gates, multiple barriers). Defence-in-depth is having a moat AND walls AND guards AND locked doors.
explanation_styles.visual: Draw the SDLC as a cycle: [Requirements] -> [Design] -> [Implementation] -> [Testing] -> [Deployment] -> [Maintenance] -> back to Requirements. At each phase, add a security gate: threat modeling at Design, code review at Implementation, penetration testing at Testing.
explanation_styles.step_by_step: Step 1: Identify what you are protecting (assets). Step 2: Identify who might attack (threat actors). Step 3: Identify how they might attack (STRIDE). Step 4: Design defences (principles). Step 5: Build with security in every layer (defence-in-depth). Step 6: Test that defences work. Step 7: Monitor and maintain.
explanation_styles.code_trace: Look at each function: Does it validate input? (Complete mediation) Does it use minimum permissions? (Least privilege) Does it fail securely? (Fail-safe defaults) Does it have one clear purpose? (Economy of mechanism) If any answer is NO, there is a design flaw.
explanation_styles.comparison: WITHOUT security architecture: developers add security as patches after breaches. WITH security architecture: security is built into every decision from day one. The first approach is like adding a lock to a cardboard door. The second is like building a steel vault.

lessons.title: Security Architecture: Building Secure Systems from the Ground Up

theory.introduction: Imagine building a house. Would you add the locks and alarms AFTER everything else is done? Of course not — you design security into the blueprints from the start. Software is the same. Security architecture means making security a FUNDAMENTAL part of how systems are designed, built, tested, and maintained. This is not about adding a firewall at the end — it is about ensuring every architectural decision considers security implications. The Secure Development Lifecycle (SDLC) provides a framework for integrating security into every phase. Threat modeling (using techniques like STRIDE) helps you think like an attacker before you write a single line of code. And secure design principles — many dating back to Saltzer and Schroeder's groundbreaking 1975 work — give you time-tested rules for building robust systems. In the real world, the most devastating breaches happen not because of a single bug, but because of ARCHITECTURAL flaws: missing authentication on critical functions, no input validation layer, single points of failure, or systems where a breach in one component exposes everything. This course teaches you to prevent those flaws at the design level.
theory.why_important: The cost of fixing a security flaw increases 100x as it moves from design to production. A design-level vulnerability — like missing access control on an entire API — cannot be fixed with a single patch; it requires redesigning the system. The Equifax breach, the SolarWinds supply chain attack, and countless others were fundamentally ARCHITECTURAL failures. Companies like Microsoft, Google, and Amazon invest heavily in threat modeling and security architecture because preventing flaws at design time is exponentially cheaper and more effective than finding them after deployment.

key_concepts.explanation: The SDLC integrates security activities into every phase of software development. Requirements phase: define security requirements and abuse cases. Design phase: threat modeling, security architecture review. Implementation phase: secure coding standards, code review. Testing phase: security testing, penetration testing, static analysis. Deployment phase: hardening, configuration review. Maintenance phase: patch management, incident response. Security is not a phase — it is a continuous thread through ALL phases.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Think of SDLC like a building inspection at every stage: foundation check, frame check, electrical check, final walkthrough. You do not wait until the house is built to check if the wiring is safe!

key_concepts.explanation: STRIDE is a framework for identifying threats, created by Microsoft. Each letter represents a threat category: Spoofing (pretending to be someone else), Tampering (modifying data), Repudiation (denying actions), Information Disclosure (leaking data), Denial of Service (making system unavailable), Elevation of Privilege (gaining unauthorised access). For each component in your system, ask: can this be Spoofed? Tampered with? etc. This systematically identifies threats you might otherwise miss.
key_concepts.ai_hint: STRIDE is like a security checklist for every part of your system. Think of it as asking six questions about every door, window, and room in your building.

key_concepts.explanation: Defence-in-depth means using multiple layers of security so that if one layer fails, others still protect the system. Like a medieval castle with a moat, outer wall, inner wall, keep, and guards at every gate — an attacker must breach ALL layers. In software: network firewall + web application firewall + input validation + parameterised queries + output encoding + access control + encryption + monitoring. No single layer is perfect, but together they create strong protection.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Never rely on a single defence. A firewall alone will not stop everything. Input validation alone will not stop everything. But TOGETHER, they create a wall that is extremely hard to breach.

key_concepts.explanation: Every user, process, and component should have only the minimum permissions necessary for its function. A web application that only reads data should connect to the database with a read-only account. An API endpoint that displays user profiles should not have access to delete users. If a component is compromised, least privilege limits the damage to only what that component could do — not everything.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Give a babysitter the house key, not the car keys and bank card. They only need access to what their job requires.

key_concepts.explanation: The default state of a system should be DENY access, not ALLOW. If a configuration is missing, if an error occurs, if a condition is not explicitly handled — the system should default to the most secure state. Whitelisting (allow known good) is more secure than blacklisting (block known bad). If your access control has a bug, it should deny everyone rather than allow everyone.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Fail-safe is like a bank vault door that locks when the power goes out, not opens. If anything goes wrong, the system should become MORE secure, not less.

key_concepts.explanation: EVERY access to EVERY resource must be checked for authorisation. Not just the first time — every single time. No caching of permissions, no assumptions that 'if they got this far, they must be authorised.' Web applications often check permissions on the page load but not on the API endpoint that the page calls. Complete mediation means checking at every layer: UI, API, service, database.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Complete mediation is like checking every guest's wristband at every door in a venue, not just at the entrance. Someone might have snuck in or had their wristband revoked.

key_concepts.explanation: Keep the design as simple as possible. Simpler systems have fewer bugs, and fewer bugs mean fewer vulnerabilities. Complex code is harder to review, harder to test, and more likely to contain hidden flaws. Prefer well-tested standard libraries over custom implementations. A custom encryption algorithm is almost certainly worse than AES. A custom session manager is likely less secure than the framework's built-in one.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Economy of mechanism is like using a well-tested lock from a reputable company rather than building your own. Your homemade lock might have flaws you cannot see.

key_concepts.explanation: No single person or component should have complete control over a critical function. In finance, the person who approves expenses should not be the same person who writes cheques. In software, the developer who writes code should not be the same person who deploys to production. This prevents both accidental errors and malicious insider actions.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Separation of duties is like having two people with different keys both needed to open a nuclear launch. No single person can act alone on critical operations.

key_concepts.explanation: The security of a system should not depend on keeping the design secret (security through obscurity). The design can be open — security comes from the strength of the mechanisms (keys, algorithms, access controls), not from hiding how they work. AES encryption is public knowledge, but it is still unbreakable because the KEYS are secret, not the algorithm. Hiding your source code does not make it secure.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Open design is like a lock where everyone can see HOW the mechanism works, but only the person with the key can open it. The lock's security comes from the key, not from hiding the lock.

key_concepts.explanation: Security mechanisms must be easy to use correctly and hard to use incorrectly. If security is too inconvenient, users will find workarounds (writing passwords on sticky notes, disabling firewalls, sharing accounts). The best security is invisible — it protects without burdening the user. Password managers, biometrics, and single sign-on are examples of making security convenient.
key_concepts.ai_hint: The best lock is one people actually use. A 10-digit combination that changes daily is technically more secure, but people will just leave the door propped open.

key_concepts.explanation: Two fundamental architectural decisions conflict: Protection (centralise assets so they are easy to guard) vs Distribution (spread assets so a single breach does not expose everything). A single database is easy to secure but catastrophic if breached. Distributed databases limit breach impact but are harder to protect. The solution is layered: centralise authentication, distribute data, encrypt everything, and monitor all access.
key_concepts.ai_hint: It is like deciding whether to keep all your money in one highly secure vault or spread it across multiple safes. The answer is usually: a bit of both, with encryption as insurance.

key_concepts.explanation: Risk assessment during design evaluates: What assets need protection? What threats exist? What is the likelihood and impact of each threat? What controls mitigate the risk? Risk = Likelihood x Impact. High-likelihood, high-impact risks get priority. Design risk assessment uses system architecture to identify vulnerabilities from design choices (e.g., choosing HTTP instead of HTTPS, using a single shared database account).
key_concepts.ai_hint: Risk assessment is like a doctor's triage: treat the most dangerous and likely conditions first. Not every risk is equal — focus resources on the ones that matter most.

key_concepts.explanation: Security patterns are proven solutions to recurring security problems. Anti-patterns are common but flawed approaches. Pattern: parameterised queries for database access. Anti-pattern: string concatenation for queries. Pattern: centralised authentication service. Anti-pattern: each microservice implementing its own auth. Pattern: structured error handling with generic user messages. Anti-pattern: showing stack traces to users. Learning patterns helps you make good design decisions quickly.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Patterns are like recipes from master chefs — proven to work. Anti-patterns are the common mistakes that lead to food poisoning. Learn both so you can spot trouble and apply solutions.

complete_examples.title: Example 1: SDLC Security Checklist
complete_examples.description: Security activities mapped to each development phase
complete_examples.output:
=== SECURE DEVELOPMENT LIFECYCLE ===

[REQUIREMENTS]
  1. Define security requirements
  2. Identify sensitive data
  3. Define access control needs
  4. List compliance requirements

[DESIGN]
  1. Threat model (STRIDE)
  2. Security architecture review
  3. Choose auth mechanism
  4. Plan defence-in-depth

[IMPLEMENTATION]
  1. Follow secure coding standards
  2. Use parameterised queries
  3. Validate all input
  4. Peer code review

[TESTING]
  1. Static analysis (SAST)
  2. Dynamic analysis (DAST)
  3. Penetration testing
  4. Dependency scanning

[DEPLOYMENT]
  1. Harden configurations
  2. Remove defaults/samples
  3. Enable HTTPS
  4. Configure monitoring

[MAINTENANCE]
  1. Patch vulnerabilities
  2. Monitor for attacks
  3. Incident response
  4. Regular security audits
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Each SDLC phase has specific security activities. Requirements defines WHAT to protect. Design defines HOW to protect. Implementation builds the protection. Testing verifies it works. Deployment configures it. Maintenance keeps it working.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Which phase do you think is most commonly skipped? Research shows design-phase security (threat modeling) is often missed. Why is this the most costly phase to skip?

complete_examples.title: Example 2: STRIDE Threat Model for a Login System
complete_examples.output:
=== STRIDE: LOGIN SYSTEM ===
[S] Spoofing
  Threat: Attacker uses stolen credentials
  Fix: MFA, account lockout, anomaly detection
  Risk: HIGH

[S] Spoofing
  Threat: Attacker creates fake login page (phishing)
  Fix: Security headers, user education, HSTS
  Risk: HIGH

... (10 threats total)
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Each STRIDE category reveals different threats. A single login page has at least 10 identifiable threats! Systematic threat modeling catches issues that ad-hoc thinking misses.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Pick another system component (e.g., file upload, payment processing) and apply STRIDE. How many threats can you find?

complete_examples.title: Example 3: Defence-in-Depth Layer Simulator
complete_examples.description: Simulating how multiple security layers stop attacks
complete_examples.output:
=== DEFENCE-IN-DEPTH SIMULATION ===
Attack: XSS via script tag
  Payload: <script>alert(1)</script>
  BLOCKED at Layer 1: WAF

Attack: SQL DROP TABLE
  Payload: '; DROP TABLE users;--
  BLOCKED at Layer 1: WAF

Attack: SQL UNION injection
  Payload: ' UNION SELECT * FROM secrets--
  BLOCKED at Layer 2: Input Validation

Attack: Normal input
  Payload: john.doe@email.com
  Layer 3: Parameterised query neutralises injection
  Result: No damage (all layers held)
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Different attacks are caught at different layers. The WAF catches known patterns, input validation catches special characters, parameterised queries prevent any remaining injection, and DB permissions limit damage. Normal input passes through all layers safely.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What if an attacker finds a way past the WAF? That is why we have MULTIPLE layers! Design an attack that bypasses layer 1 but is caught by layer 2.

complete_examples.title: Example 4: Fail-Safe Defaults in Access Control
complete_examples.description: Comparing blacklist (default allow) vs whitelist (default deny)
complete_examples.output:
=== FAIL-SAFE DEFAULTS ===
Role       Blacklist    Whitelist   
ADMIN      ALLOW        ALLOW       
USER       ALLOW        ALLOW       
BANNED     DENY         DENY        
HACKER     ALLOW        DENY        
null       DENY         DENY        
(empty)    ALLOW        DENY        
UNKNOWN    ALLOW        DENY
complete_examples.ai_explanation: The blacklist allows HACKER, empty string, and UNKNOWN because they are not on the banned list. The whitelist denies everything not explicitly allowed. In security, default-deny (whitelist) is always safer.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Write a whitelist for HTTP methods that only allows GET and POST. What happens when someone sends a DELETE or PATCH request?

complete_examples.title: Example 5: Complete Mediation Checker
complete_examples.description: Verifying every endpoint has authentication and authorisation
complete_examples.output:
=== COMPLETE MEDIATION AUDIT ===
[OK]   GET / (public)
[OK]   GET /login (public)
[OK]   POST /login (public)
[OK]   GET /profile
[OK]   GET /admin
[OK]   POST /transfer
[FAIL] DELETE /user - MISSING AUTHORISATION!
[FAIL] GET /api/data - MISSING AUTHENTICATION!

Issues found: 2
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Complete mediation means EVERY non-public endpoint must have both authentication (who are you?) and authorisation (what can you do?). The audit found two gaps: DELETE /user has auth but no authz (any logged-in user can delete), and GET /api/data has neither (anyone can access).
complete_examples.ai_challenge: How would you automate this audit as part of your CI/CD pipeline? Think about how to ensure new endpoints always have security checks.

complete_examples.title: Example 6: Risk Assessment Calculator
complete_examples.description: Quantifying security risks with likelihood and impact
complete_examples.output:
=== DESIGN RISK ASSESSMENT ===
L=Likelihood(1-5) I=Impact(1-5) Score=LxI

[!!!] SQL Injection                    L=4 I=5 Score=20 CRITICAL
[!!!] Broken Authentication            L=4 I=5 Score=20 CRITICAL
[!! ] Sensitive Data Exposure           L=3 I=5 Score=15 HIGH
[!! ] XSS (Stored)                     L=4 I=4 Score=16 HIGH
[!! ] CSRF                             L=3 I=4 Score=12 HIGH
[!  ] Insecure Deserialization         L=2 I=5 Score=10 MEDIUM
[!  ] DDoS Attack                      L=3 I=3 Score=9  MEDIUM
[!  ] Information Leakage (errors)     L=4 I=2 Score=8  MEDIUM
[   ] Clickjacking                     L=2 I=2 Score=4  LOW
[   ] DNS Misconfiguration             L=1 I=3 Score=3  LOW
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Risk = Likelihood x Impact. SQL Injection and Broken Auth are CRITICAL because they are both likely AND devastating. Clickjacking is LOW because it is less likely and lower impact. This prioritises which threats to address first.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: After adding mitigations (parameterised queries for SQL injection), reassess the likelihood. How does it change the risk score?

complete_examples.title: Example 7: Economy of Mechanism Comparison
complete_examples.description: Comparing complex custom code vs simple standard library usage
complete_examples.output:
=== ECONOMY OF MECHANISM ===

Custom XOR 'encryption':
  Encrypted: (garbled text)
  Decrypted: password
  Lines of code: ~10
  Security level: NONE (trivially breakable)
  Bugs likely: XOR is symmetric, key reuse, no IV

Standard library AES:
  Cipher c = Cipher.getInstance("AES/GCM/NoPadding");
  Lines of code: ~5
  Security level: Military grade (AES-256)
  Bugs likely: ZERO (millions of users, expert review)

LESSON: Less custom code = fewer bugs = better security
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Custom XOR 'encryption' is trivially breakable but looks like it works. Standard AES is truly secure and requires LESS code. Economy of mechanism: use proven tools, keep it simple.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Research the Schneier's Law: 'Anyone can create an encryption algorithm they themselves cannot break.' Why does this make custom crypto dangerous?

complete_examples.title: Example 8: Separation of Duties Simulator
complete_examples.description: Enforcing that no single person controls an entire process
complete_examples.output:
=== SEPARATION OF DUTIES ===

Transfer $50000 submitted by alice
Transfer $50000 approved by bob

Attempt self-approval:
Transfer $100000 submitted by eve
DENIED: Approver cannot be the same as submitter!
complete_examples.ai_explanation: The person who submits a transfer cannot approve it. This prevents both fraud (insider threat) and mistakes (accidental transfers). The check is simple: submitter != approver.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: For very high-value transfers, companies require TWO approvers. How would you implement dual-approval?

complete_examples.title: Example 9: Threat Model Document Generator
complete_examples.description: Creating a structured threat model for a web application
complete_examples.output:
=====================================
  THREAT MODEL: Online Banking App
=====================================

1. SYSTEM DESCRIPTION
   Web app for balance checks, transfers, payments
   Components: Browser, Web Server, App Server, Database

2. ASSETS
   - Customer credentials
   - Financial data
   - Personal data
   - System availability

3. THREAT ACTORS
   - External attackers
   - Malicious insiders
   - Automated bots
   - Nation-state actors

4. TRUST BOUNDARIES
   [Internet] <--boundary--> [DMZ]
   [DMZ] <--boundary--> [Internal Network]
   [App Server] <--boundary--> [Database]

5. STRIDE THREATS (Top 5)
...

6. MITIGATIONS
...
complete_examples.ai_explanation: A threat model documents: what we protect (assets), who attacks (actors), where boundaries exist (trust boundaries), what can go wrong (STRIDE), and how we defend (mitigations). This is created BEFORE coding begins.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add a section 7 (Residual Risk) that lists risks that remain even after mitigations. What cannot be fully prevented?

complete_examples.title: Example 10: Layered Architecture Security
complete_examples.description: Security controls at each architectural layer
complete_examples.output:
=== LAYERED ARCHITECTURE SECURITY ===

Request 1: XSS attempt
  UI:     BLOCKED: XSS attempt in UI layer

Request 2: Unauthorised admin action
  UI:     PASS
  App:    BLOCKED: Unauthorised

Request 3: SQL injection that bypasses app layer
  UI:     PASS
  App:    PASS
  Data:   BLOCKED: Dangerous query

Request 4: Legitimate read
  UI:     PASS
  App:    PASS
  Data:   PASS (using parameterised query)
  DB:     PASS
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Each layer catches different attacks. XSS is caught at the UI layer. Unauthorised actions at the app layer. SQL injection at the data layer. And even if all three fail, the database permissions provide a final defence.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What happens if Request 3 also bypasses the data layer? The DB layer would still block DROP because the app uses a readonly user. That is defence-in-depth!

complete_examples.title: Example 11: Secure Design Review Checklist
complete_examples.description: Evaluating a system design against all secure design principles
complete_examples.output:
=== SECURE DESIGN REVIEW ===

[PASS] Least Privilege: DB connections use minimum permissions?
[PASS] Least Privilege: API endpoints have role-based access?
[PASS] Fail-Safe: Default access is DENY (whitelist)?
[FAIL] Fail-Safe: Errors default to secure state?
[FAIL] Complete Mediation: Every endpoint checks auth+authz?
[PASS] Complete Mediation: API and UI have same access controls?
[PASS] Economy: Using framework auth, not custom?
[PASS] Economy: Using standard crypto libraries?
[PASS] Separation: Different roles for submit/approve?
[PASS] Open Design: Security does not rely on hidden URLs?
[FAIL] Psychological: Security mechanisms are user-friendly?
[PASS] Defence-in-Depth: Multiple layers for each threat?

Score: 9/12 - NEEDS WORK
complete_examples.ai_explanation: This checklist applies all Saltzer & Schroeder principles plus modern additions. Three failures: errors don't fail safely, not all endpoints are mediated, and security mechanisms annoy users. Each failure is a design flaw to fix BEFORE coding.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: How would you fix the three failures? For complete mediation, consider using a middleware/filter that runs on EVERY request.

complete_examples.title: Example 12: Architecture Security Anti-Patterns
complete_examples.description: Common architectural mistakes and their secure alternatives
complete_examples.output:
=== ARCHITECTURE ANTI-PATTERNS ===

Shared Database Account:
  ANTI-PATTERN: All services use the same root DB account
  PATTERN:      Each service has own limited account
  RISK:         One compromised service = total DB access

Security by Obscurity:
  ANTI-PATTERN: Hide admin panel at /s3cr3t-admin
  PATTERN:      Protect admin panel with authentication + RBAC
  RISK:         URL discovery exposes admin panel

... (6 anti-patterns total)
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Each anti-pattern represents a common architectural mistake. The pattern is the proven secure alternative. Understanding both helps you make better design decisions.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Can you think of a 7th anti-pattern? Hint: think about logging, error handling, or dependency management.

complete_examples.title: Example 13: Trust Boundary Analyser
complete_examples.description: Identifying and securing trust boundaries in architecture
complete_examples.output:
=== TRUST BOUNDARY ANALYSIS ===

BOUNDARY: Internet -> Web Server
  Risk: User input crosses from untrusted internet
  Controls: WAF, HTTPS, input validation, rate limiting

BOUNDARY: Browser -> Server API
  Risk: Client-side data reaches server
  Controls: Server-side validation, CSRF tokens, auth headers

... (6 boundaries)

RULE: Validate ALL data crossing any trust boundary.
RULE: Never trust data from a less-trusted zone.
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Every boundary where data flows from a less-trusted zone to a more-trusted zone needs security controls. The internet is least trusted, the database is most trusted. Each crossing point needs specific protections.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Draw (on paper or in code) the trust boundaries for YOUR project. Where does untrusted data enter? Where are the boundaries?

complete_examples.title: Example 14: Real-World Architecture Failure Analysis
complete_examples.description: Analysing famous breaches through the lens of secure design principles
complete_examples.output:
=== ARCHITECTURE FAILURE ANALYSIS ===

Equifax 2017 (147M records exposed)
  Attack: Unpatched Apache Struts vulnerability
  Principle violated: SDLC: No patch management in maintenance phase
  Lesson: Defence-in-depth: No network segmentation after web server

Capital One 2019 (100M customer records)
  Attack: SSRF attack on misconfigured WAF
  Principle violated: Least Privilege: WAF role had access to all data
  Lesson: Separation: WAF could access customer data directly

SolarWinds 2020 (18,000 organisations compromised)
  Attack: Supply chain attack via build system
  Principle violated: Complete Mediation: Build pipeline not verified
  Lesson: Defence-in-depth: No code signing verification

Heartbleed 2014 (OpenSSL memory leak)
  Attack: Buffer over-read in heartbeat extension
  Principle violated: Economy of Mechanism: Complex C code
  Lesson: Open Design: Despite open source, bug existed 2 years
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Every major breach maps to one or more violated design principles. Equifax = SDLC failure. Capital One = least privilege violation. SolarWinds = complete mediation gap. Heartbleed = complexity (economy of mechanism). These principles prevent real-world catastrophes.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Pick a recent breach and analyse which secure design principles were violated. How would applying those principles have prevented it?

visual_diagram.title: Security Architecture: SDLC, STRIDE, Design Principles & Defence-in-Depth
visual_diagram.content: [SECURE DEVELOPMENT LIFECYCLE] REQUIREMENTS Security requirements, abuse cases, data classification | ^ v | DESIGN Threat modeling (STRIDE), architecture review, design principles | | v | IMPLEMENT Secure coding, code review, approved libraries | | v | TEST SAST, DAST, pen testing, dependency scanning | | v | DEPLOY Hardening, HTTPS, remove defaults, enable monitoring | | v | MAINTAIN Patching, monitoring, incident response -----> BACK TO TOP [STRIDE THREAT CATEGORIES] S = Spoofing (fake identity) Fix: Strong authentication T = Tampering (modify data) Fix: Integrity checks, HTTPS R = Repudiation (deny actions) Fix: Audit logging I = Info Disclosure (leak data) Fix: Encryption, access control D = Denial of Service (crash system) Fix: Rate limiting, redundancy E = Elevation of Privilege (gain access) Fix: RBAC, least privilege [DESIGN PRINCIPLES (Saltzer & Schroeder 1975)] 1 Least Privilege - minimum permissions needed 2 Fail-Safe Defaults - deny by default 3 Complete Mediation - check every access 4 Economy of Mechanism - keep it simple 5 Separation of Duties - no single point of control 6 Open Design - security ≠ secrecy 7 Psychological Acceptability - usable security 8 Defence-in-Depth - multiple layers [DEFENCE-IN-DEPTH LAYERS] Layer 6: Monitoring & Logging Layer 5: Database Permissions (Least Privilege) Layer 4: Parameterised Queries / Safe APIs Layer 3: Input Validation at Trust Boundaries Layer 2: Authentication + Authorisation Layer 1: Network Firewall + WAF + HTTPS

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 1: SDLC Phase Matching
practice_exercises.description: Match security activities to SDLC phases.
practice_exercises.expected_output: 1.DESIGN 2.MAINTENANCE 3.TESTING 4.REQUIREMENTS 5.IMPLEMENTATION 6.DEPLOYMENT 7.IMPLEMENTATION 8.REQUIREMENTS 9.TESTING 10.MAINTENANCE

hints.message: Think about WHEN each activity happens in the development process.
Threat modeling is design-time. Patches are post-deployment. Pen testing is before release.
1.DESIGN 2.MAINTENANCE 3.TESTING 4.REQUIREMENTS 5.IMPLEMENTATION 6.DEPLOYMENT 7.IMPLEMENTATION 8.REQUIREMENTS 9.TESTING 10.MAINTENANCE

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 2: Security Requirements Writing
practice_exercises.description: Write security requirements for a web app.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
SR-001: All users must authenticate with password + optional MFA
SR-002: Customer payment data must be encrypted at rest (AES-256)
SR-003: Admin functions require ADMIN role
SR-004: All authentication events must be logged with timestamps
SR-005: All data in transit must use TLS 1.2+

hints.message: Think about WHO can access WHAT and HOW data is protected.
Cover: authentication method, data encryption, access control, audit logging, transport security.
5 requirements covering: authn, encryption at rest, RBAC, audit logs, TLS.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 3: Abuse Case Identification
practice_exercises.description: Write abuse cases alongside normal use cases.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Abuse 1: Attacker brute-forces login with common passwords
Abuse 2: Attacker changes account ID to view other balances (IDOR)
Abuse 3: Attacker forges transfer request (CSRF) or changes amount
Abuse 4: Attacker impersonates admin to reset victim's password
Abuse 5: Attacker uploads malicious file (web shell, malware)

hints.message: For each normal action, think: how could an attacker misuse this?
Think about: guessing, forging, bypassing, manipulating, injecting.
Each abuse case inverts the use case: brute force, IDOR, CSRF/tampering, impersonation, malicious upload.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 4: Data Classification
practice_exercises.description: Classify application data by sensitivity level.
practice_exercises.expected_output: 1.PUBLIC 2.CONFIDENTIAL 3.CONFIDENTIAL 4.PUBLIC 5.SECRET 6.INTERNAL 7.CONFIDENTIAL 8.SECRET

hints.message: PUBLIC: anyone can see. SECRET: highest protection. INTERNAL: employees only. CONFIDENTIAL: restricted.
Credit cards and encryption keys are SECRET. Passwords (even hashed) and source code are CONFIDENTIAL.
1.PUBLIC 2.CONFIDENTIAL 3.CONFIDENTIAL 4.PUBLIC 5.SECRET 6.INTERNAL 7.CONFIDENTIAL 8.SECRET

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 5: Compliance Requirements
practice_exercises.description: Identify which regulations apply to a system.
practice_exercises.expected_output: 1.PCI-DSS 2.HIPAA 3.GDPR 4.SOX 5.ISO 27001

hints.message: PCI-DSS = payment cards. HIPAA = health. GDPR = EU privacy. SOX = finance. ISO 27001 = general.
Credit cards -> PCI-DSS. Medical -> HIPAA. EU residents -> GDPR.
1.PCI-DSS 2.HIPAA 3.GDPR 4.SOX 5.ISO 27001

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 6: SDLC Cost of Fixing Bugs
practice_exercises.description: Calculate the cost multiplier of late-found security bugs.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Finding a missing auth requirement:
  Requirements: $100
  Design: $500
  Implementation: $1000
  Testing: $2000
  Production: $10000

hints.message: Multiply baseCost by each multiplier.
baseCost * multipliers[i] for each phase.
Just multiply and print. The lesson: fix early, save money.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 7: Security Gate Checklist
practice_exercises.description: Create go/no-go criteria for each SDLC phase.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Requirements->Design: Security requirements documented? Threat actors identified?
Design->Implementation: Threat model complete? Architecture reviewed?
Implementation->Testing: Code reviewed? No high-severity SAST findings?
Testing->Deployment: Pen test passed? All critical vulnerabilities fixed?

hints.message: What must be TRUE before moving to the next phase?
Each gate has criteria: documentation, review, testing results.
2 criteria per gate: doc/review for early gates, test results for later gates.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 8: Security vs Feature Trade-off
practice_exercises.description: Evaluate when security impacts user experience.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1.GOOD (reasonable timeout)
2.TRADE-OFF (too strict, users will write it down)
3.GOOD (strong security, acceptable UX)
4.BAD (no security)
5.GOOD (better UX AND security)
6.TRADE-OFF (too annoying, users will find workarounds)

hints.message: Good security is usable. If it is too strict, users bypass it.
30min timeout is reasonable. 40-char password is excessive. SSO improves both.
1.GOOD 2.TRADE-OFF 3.GOOD 4.BAD 5.GOOD 6.TRADE-OFF

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 9: Maintenance Phase Activities
practice_exercises.description: Plan ongoing security activities post-deployment.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Week 1: Apply security patches and dependency updates
Week 2: Review security logs and alerts for anomalies
Week 3: Run automated vulnerability scan
Week 4: Review access controls and remove unused accounts

hints.message: What security tasks should happen regularly after deployment?
Patching, log review, scanning, access review are the four pillars.
Week1: patches. Week2: log review. Week3: vuln scan. Week4: access review.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 10: Order the SDLC
practice_exercises.description: Put security activities in correct SDLC order.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. Identify compliance requirements (REQUIREMENTS)
2. Define data classification (REQUIREMENTS)
3. Choose authentication mechanism (DESIGN)
4. Threat modeling (DESIGN)
5. Security code review (IMPLEMENTATION)
6. Static code analysis (TESTING)
7. Penetration testing (TESTING)
8. Deploy with HTTPS (DEPLOYMENT)
9. Monitor for attacks (MAINTENANCE)
10. Apply security patches (MAINTENANCE)

hints.message: Group by phase: requirements first, then design, implementation, testing, deployment, maintenance.
Requirements activities come before design. Testing before deployment. Maintenance is last.
Compliance+classification -> authn+threat model -> code review+SAST -> pen test -> deploy -> monitor+patch

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 11: STRIDE Category Quiz
practice_exercises.description: Match threats to STRIDE categories.
practice_exercises.expected_output: 1.I (Information Disclosure) 2.D (Denial of Service) 3.T (Tampering) 4.S (Spoofing) 5.R (Repudiation) 6.E (Elevation of Privilege)

hints.message: S=fake identity, T=modify data, R=deny action, I=leak data, D=crash, E=gain access.
Unencrypted read=I, flood=D, modify=T, stolen cookie=S, deny action=R, unauthorised access=E.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 12: STRIDE a File Upload
practice_exercises.description: Apply STRIDE to a file upload feature.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
S: Attacker uploads as another user (spoofed session)
T: Attacker modifies file contents after upload
R: Uploader denies uploading malicious file
I: Uploaded files accessible to unauthorised users
D: Uploading very large files crashes the server
E: Upload a web shell to gain server access

hints.message: Think about each STRIDE letter applied to uploading a file.
Who uploads? Can files be changed? Can they deny it? Can others see it? Can it crash things? Can it grant access?
S:spoofed user T:modified file R:deny upload I:unauthorised access D:huge file E:web shell

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 13: STRIDE a Chat Application
practice_exercises.description: Threat model a real-time messaging system.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
S: Impersonate another user in chat -> MFA + verified usernames
T: Modify message after sending -> Message integrity hashes
R: Deny sending a threatening message -> Immutable audit log
I: Read private messages -> End-to-end encryption
D: Spam flood crashes chat server -> Rate limiting per user
E: Regular user gains moderator powers -> Server-side role checks

hints.message: Think about each STRIDE category in context of sending/receiving messages.
S:impersonation T:message modification R:deny sending I:eavesdropping D:spam E:role escalation
Full STRIDE with mitigations for each category.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 14: Build a STRIDE Table
practice_exercises.description: Create a structured threat table for an API endpoint.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
S | Stolen credentials | HIGH | MEDIUM | MFA + lockout
T | Modified amount in transit | HIGH | LOW | HTTPS + server validation
R | Deny initiating transfer | MEDIUM | MEDIUM | Audit log + confirmation
I | Transfer details leaked | HIGH | LOW | Encryption + access control
D | Flood transfer requests | MEDIUM | HIGH | Rate limiting
E | User transfers from other account | CRITICAL | MEDIUM | Ownership check

hints.message: For each STRIDE category, think about the bank transfer endpoint specifically.
What can be spoofed? What data can be tampered? What action denied? What leaked? What flooded? What escalated?
Table with 6 rows, one per STRIDE category with threat, impact, likelihood, and mitigation.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 15: Identify Trust Boundaries
practice_exercises.description: Find where trust levels change in a system.
practice_exercises.expected_output: 1.YES (untrusted client -> server) 2.NO (same trust level) 3.YES (app -> data) 4.YES (internal -> external) 5.YES (client -> server) 6.NO (same process)

hints.message: Trust boundaries exist where data crosses between different trust levels.
Browser->server is always a boundary. Internal function->function is not. External APIs are boundaries.
1.YES 2.NO 3.YES 4.YES 5.YES 6.NO

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 16: Threat Actor Profiling
practice_exercises.description: Identify and characterise threat actors.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. Skill=L Resources=L Motivation=Fun/reputation
2. Skill=H Resources=H Motivation=Financial gain
3. Skill=H Resources=H Motivation=Espionage/disruption
4. Skill=M Resources=M Motivation=Revenge/sabotage
5. Skill=M Resources=L Motivation=Political/social cause

hints.message: Different attackers have different capabilities and motivations.
Script kiddies use pre-built tools. Nation-states have unlimited resources. Insiders have existing access.
1.L/L/fun 2.H/H/money 3.H/H/espionage 4.M/M/revenge 5.M/L/political

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 17: Mitigation Mapping
practice_exercises.description: Map threats to appropriate security controls.
practice_exercises.expected_output: 1.B (Rate limiting stops brute force) 2.D (Input validation + parameterised queries) 3.C (Encryption protects stored data) 4.F (RBAC restricts admin functions) 5.E (Audit logging proves the action) 6.A (MFA stops stolen credentials)

hints.message: Match each threat to the control that most directly prevents it.
Brute force -> rate limit. Injection -> validation. Stolen data -> encryption. Unauthorised -> RBAC. Deny -> logging. Stolen creds -> MFA.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 18: STRIDE for REST API
practice_exercises.description: Comprehensive threat model for API endpoints.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
GET /api/users:
  [I] User list exposed to unauthorised users -> Add auth + RBAC
  [D] Requesting huge user list crashes server -> Pagination + rate limit
POST /api/users:
  [S] Attacker creates account as another person -> Email verification
  [T] Attacker modifies user data in request -> Server-side validation
DELETE /api/users/{id}:
  [E] Regular user deletes admin account -> RBAC + ownership check
  [R] Admin denies deleting a user -> Audit logging

hints.message: For each endpoint, think: who should NOT access it? What data can be stolen or modified?
GET: info disclosure + DoS. POST: spoofing + tampering. DELETE: elevation + repudiation.
2 threats per endpoint from different STRIDE categories.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 19: Attack Tree Construction
practice_exercises.description: Build an attack tree for a security goal.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
GOAL: Access admin panel
  Path 1: Steal admin credentials
    1a. Phishing email to admin
    1b. Brute force admin password
    1c. Find credentials in source code
  Path 2: Bypass authentication
    2a. SQL injection in login form
    2b. Session fixation attack
    2c. Exploit password reset flaw
  Path 3: Escalate privileges
    3a. Modify role in cookie/token
    3b. Access admin URL directly (missing auth check)
    3c. Exploit IDOR to change own role

hints.message: Think of different WAYS an attacker could reach the goal.
Three main paths: steal credentials, bypass auth, escalate privileges. Each has sub-techniques.
3 paths with 3 sub-steps each: credential theft, auth bypass, privilege escalation.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 20: Data Flow Diagram Security
practice_exercises.description: Identify threats along data flows.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Browser->LB: Data in transit unencrypted (need HTTPS/TLS)
LB->Web: Internal traffic may be unencrypted (need internal TLS)
Web->App: Unvalidated input forwarded (need input validation)
App->DB: SQL injection if queries not parameterised (need prepared statements)

hints.message: At each hop, data crosses a potential trust boundary.
Each hop can have: encryption, validation, injection, or access control issues.
HTTPS for transit, internal TLS, input validation before app server, parameterised queries for DB.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 21: Identify the Principle
practice_exercises.description: Match code examples to design principles.
practice_exercises.expected_output: 1.Fail-Safe Defaults 2.Least Privilege 3.Economy of Mechanism 4.Complete Mediation 5.Separation of Duties 6.Open Design

hints.message: Whitelist = fail-safe. Minimum permissions = least privilege. Standard library = economy.
1.Fail-safe(whitelist) 2.LP(minimum access) 3.Economy(standard lib) 4.CM(every call) 5.Separation 6.Open.
1.Fail-Safe 2.Least Privilege 3.Economy 4.Complete Mediation 5.Separation 6.Open Design

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 22: Implement Fail-Safe Config
practice_exercises.description: Build a configuration loader that defaults to secure values.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Max attempts: 5
SSL enabled: true
Debug mode: false

hints.message: If config has the key, use it. Otherwise, use the secure default.
return config.getOrDefault(key, secureDefault);
config.getOrDefault(key, secureDefault) - SSL defaults ON, debug defaults OFF.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 23: Complete Mediation Filter
practice_exercises.description: Build a middleware that checks every request.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
ALLOW: /login (public)
401: /profile requires authentication
ALLOW: /profile (USER)
ALLOW: /admin (USER) [Note: authz check needed separately]

hints.message: Check if path is public first. Then check session.
if(publicPaths.contains(path)) allow. else if(sessionId==null or invalid) 401. else allow.
Public path -> allow. No/invalid session -> 401. Valid session -> allow (authz is separate).

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 24: Least Privilege DB Users
practice_exercises.description: Design database users with minimum permissions.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Search Service: SELECT on products, categories
Order Service: SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE on orders, inventory
Admin Service: ALL on users, settings, orders
Backup Service: SELECT on ALL tables (read-only)
Report Service: SELECT on orders, users (read-only, aggregates only)

hints.message: Each service only needs permissions for ITS specific tasks.
Search=read products. Orders=read+write orders. Admin=everything. Backup=read all. Reports=read summaries.
Minimum permissions per service function.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 25: Economy: Custom vs Library
practice_exercises.description: Compare custom code vs standard library for security tasks.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1.CUSTOM (risky! use bcrypt library)
2.LIBRARY (good!)
3.LIBRARY (good!)
4.CUSTOM (risky! use AES)
5.CUSTOM (risky! use SecureRandom)
6.LIBRARY (good!)

hints.message: Standard libraries are tested by millions. Custom security code likely has bugs.
MD5 is weak. XOR is breakable. Math.random() is predictable. Use established libraries.
1.CUSTOM(bad) 2.LIBRARY 3.LIBRARY 4.CUSTOM(bad) 5.CUSTOM(bad) 6.LIBRARY

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 26: Separation of Duties Checker
practice_exercises.description: Verify that no single user controls a full process.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
OK: All different (alice, bob, charlie)
VIOLATION: submitter=approver (alice)
VIOLATION: submitter=deployer (alice)

hints.message: Check all three pairs: submitter!=approver, submitter!=deployer, approver!=deployer.
Three checks: s!=a, s!=d, a!=d. If any match, report violation.
Check all pairs for equality. Report which roles overlap.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 27: Open Design Test
practice_exercises.description: Verify security doesn't rely on obscurity.
practice_exercises.expected_output: 1.OBSCURITY 2.OPEN DESIGN 3.OBSCURITY 4.OPEN DESIGN 5.OPEN DESIGN 6.OBSCURITY

hints.message: If the security breaks when the attacker learns the mechanism, it is obscurity.
Hidden URLs, custom encoding, unusual ports = obscurity. Auth, encryption, RBAC = open design.
1.OBSCURITY 2.OPEN 3.OBSCURITY 4.OPEN 5.OPEN 6.OBSCURITY

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 28: Defence-in-Depth for XSS
practice_exercises.description: Layer multiple defences against cross-site scripting.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Layer 1: Content-Security-Policy header (blocks inline scripts)
Layer 2: Input validation (reject/sanitise HTML)
Layer 3: Output encoding (escape < > & " ')
Layer 4: HttpOnly cookies (JS cannot steal session)
Layer 5: Framework auto-escaping (template engines escape by default)

hints.message: Think of multiple independent defences against XSS.
CSP header, input validation, output encoding, HttpOnly cookies, template auto-escaping.
5 layers: CSP, validation, encoding, HttpOnly, auto-escaping.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 29: Psychological Acceptability Review
practice_exercises.description: Evaluate if security is user-friendly.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1.ACCEPTABLE (convenient biometric)
2.UNACCEPTABLE (too strict, users will write it down)
3.ACCEPTABLE (one login for everything)
4.UNACCEPTABLE (frustrating, users leave)
5.ACCEPTABLE (easy + secure)
6.ACCEPTABLE (reasonable frequency)

hints.message: If users will bypass it or complain, it is unacceptable.
Biometrics and SSO are convenient. Excessive passwords and CAPTCHAs frustrate users.
1.OK 2.BAD 3.OK 4.BAD 5.OK 6.OK

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 30: Apply All Principles
practice_exercises.description: Review code against all design principles simultaneously.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Root DB connection: Least Privilege
Custom XOR: Economy of Mechanism
Auth only first request: Complete Mediation
Stack trace on error: Fail-Safe Defaults
Single admin all roles: Separation of Duties
Hidden admin URL: Open Design

hints.message: Match each flaw to the principle it violates.
Root=LP, Custom crypto=Economy, First-only auth=CM, Stack trace=Fail-Safe, One admin=Separation, Hidden URL=Open.
LP, Economy, CM, Fail-Safe, Separation, Open Design

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 31: Layered Security Architecture
practice_exercises.description: Design security controls for each application layer.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Network: Firewall + IDS/IPS
Presentation: Input validation + CSP headers
Business Logic: Authentication + RBAC
Data Access: Parameterised queries + ORM
Database: Encryption at rest + least privilege accounts

hints.message: Each layer has different security concerns and controls.
Network=firewall. Presentation=validation. Logic=auth. Data=queries. DB=encryption.
2 controls per layer covering the main threats at each level.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 32: Microservice Security Design
practice_exercises.description: Secure inter-service communication.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. API Gateway with centralised authentication
2. mTLS (mutual TLS) between services
3. Secrets management vault (not env vars in code)
4. Centralised logging with correlation IDs
5. Service mesh with least-privilege policies

hints.message: Microservices need: central auth, encrypted communication, secret management, logging, access control.
API gateway, mTLS, vault, central logging, service mesh.
5 controls: gateway, mTLS, vault, logging, service mesh.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 33: Security Zone Design
practice_exercises.description: Define network security zones and rules.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
INTERNET->DMZ: ALLOW (web server)
INTERNET->APP_ZONE: DENY (no direct access)
DMZ->APP_ZONE: ALLOW (proxy requests)
APP_ZONE->DB_ZONE: ALLOW (data queries)
DB_ZONE->INTERNET: DENY (no outbound)
MANAGEMENT->ALL: ALLOW (admin access via VPN)

hints.message: Traffic flows inward: internet->DMZ->app->DB. No outbound from DB.
Internet only reaches DMZ. App zone only from DMZ. DB only from app. Management is special.
Inward flow only. No direct internet to app or DB. Management via VPN.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 34: Error Handling Architecture
practice_exercises.description: Design a centralised error handling strategy.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Production: An error occurred. Please try again. (Reference: ERR-20260223-001)
Development: SQL error: table users, column password

hints.message: Production shows generic message with reference ID. Development shows full details.
if(isProduction) return generic + reference ID. else return e.getMessage().
Production: generic + ID. Development: full details. Log full details server-side always.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 35: Input Validation Architecture
practice_exercises.description: Design a multi-layer validation strategy.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Client: PASS (3-20 chars, alphanumeric)
API: PASS (3-20 chars, alphanumeric, not reserved, not in blacklist)
DB: PASS (UNIQUE constraint, VARCHAR(20))

hints.message: Three layers: client for UX, API for security, DB for data integrity.
Client: length+chars. API: same + reserved words + blacklist. DB: unique + length constraint.
Client=length+pattern. API=same+reserved+blacklist. DB=unique+type constraint.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 36: Authentication Architecture
practice_exercises.description: Design centralised authentication for multiple services.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. User -> Auth Server: username + password + MFA
2. Auth Server -> User: JWT token (signed, 1-hour expiry)
3. User -> Service A: Authorization: Bearer <token>
4. Service A -> Auth Server: Validate token signature
5. Auth Server -> Service A: Valid, user=alice, role=USER
6. Service A: Check role has permission for requested action

hints.message: Centralised auth: one login, token used everywhere, each service validates.
Auth server issues JWT. Services validate the signature. No service stores passwords.
Central auth server, JWT tokens, service-level validation, no password storage in services.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 37: Logging Architecture
practice_exercises.description: Design a secure, centralised logging system.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
LOG: auth events, access denials, data changes, admin actions, errors
STORE: centralised SIEM, immutable storage, 90-day retention
PROTECT: TLS transport, access control on logs, tamper detection
NEVER LOG: passwords, credit cards, session tokens, personal data (GDPR)

hints.message: Log security events. Store securely. Never log secrets.
Log events: auth, access, changes. Store: centralised+immutable. Never: passwords, tokens, PII.
What to log, where to store, how to protect, what to exclude.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 38: Encryption Architecture
practice_exercises.description: Design data encryption at rest and in transit.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
In Transit: TLS 1.3 for all connections
At Rest (DB): AES-256 column-level encryption for sensitive fields
At Rest (Files): AES-256 full disk encryption + file-level for secrets
In Memory: Clear sensitive data after use, avoid logging
Key Management: HSM or cloud KMS, key rotation every 90 days

hints.message: Different encryption strategies for different data states.
Transit=TLS. DB=column encryption. Files=disk+file. Memory=clear after use. Keys=HSM+rotation.
TLS for transit, AES-256 for storage, clear memory, HSM for keys.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 39: Secure API Gateway Design
practice_exercises.description: Design a gateway that enforces security for all APIs.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
PASS: Authenticated, rate OK, input valid -> forward to service
401: No token provided
PASS: Authenticated, rate OK -> forward (IP logged for monitoring)

hints.message: Gateway checks in order: rate limit, auth, validate, log, forward.
Check each condition in sequence. Any failure returns error. Success forwards to backend.
Rate limit -> auth check -> input validation -> log -> forward to service.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 40: Incident Response Architecture
practice_exercises.description: Design system components for incident detection and response.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. SIEM: Centralised log collection + correlation + alerting
2. IDS/IPS: Network intrusion detection at trust boundaries
3. WAF: Application-level attack detection and blocking
4. Automated Response: Auto-block IPs after threshold + alert team
5. Forensics Storage: Immutable log archive for investigation

hints.message: What systems do you need to detect, respond to, and investigate incidents?
SIEM for correlation, IDS for detection, WAF for blocking, automation for speed, forensics for investigation.
5 components: SIEM, IDS/IPS, WAF, automated response, forensics storage.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 41: Risk Score Calculator
practice_exercises.description: Build a risk scoring system.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
SQL Injection: 20 (CRITICAL)
Clickjacking: 4 (LOW)
Account Takeover: 15 (HIGH)

hints.message: Multiply likelihood * impact, then classify.
int s = l*i; if s>=20 CRITICAL else if s>=12 HIGH else if s>=6 MEDIUM else LOW.
return threat+": "+s+" ("+(s>=20?"CRITICAL":s>=12?"HIGH":s>=6?"MEDIUM":"LOW")+")";

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 42: Risk Mitigation Priority
practice_exercises.description: Prioritise risks and assign mitigations.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Priority 1: SQL Injection (20 CRITICAL) -> Parameterised queries
Priority 2: XSS (16 HIGH) -> Output encoding + CSP
Priority 3: CSRF (12 HIGH) -> CSRF tokens + SameSite
Priority 4: Info Leak (8 MEDIUM) -> Generic errors + access control
Priority 5: Clickjacking (4 LOW) -> X-Frame-Options header

hints.message: Sort by score descending. Assign most critical first.
Highest score = highest priority. Each gets a specific mitigation.
Order by score, assign appropriate mitigation for each.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 43: Residual Risk Assessment
practice_exercises.description: Calculate remaining risk after mitigations.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
SQL Injection: Original=20(CRITICAL) -> Parameterised queries -> Residual=5(LOW)
Brute Force: Original=15(HIGH) -> Account lockout -> Residual=6(MEDIUM)

hints.message: Mitigations reduce likelihood or impact. Recalculate after applying controls.
Parameterised queries reduce SQL injection likelihood from 4 to 1. Lockout reduces brute force from 5 to 2.
Recalculate: newL * I = residual. Mitigations primarily reduce likelihood.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 44: Threat Model Document
practice_exercises.description: Create a complete threat model for a project.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
THREAT MODEL: E-Commerce Platform
1. System: Web app for shopping
2. Assets: Customer PII, Payment data, Inventory
3. Actors: Hackers, Insiders, Bots
4. STRIDE: [6 threats with scores]
5. Mitigations: [matched to threats]
6. Residual risks: [remaining exposure]

hints.message: Follow the template: describe, identify assets, actors, threats, score, mitigate.
6 sections covering the full threat model lifecycle.
Complete document with all 6 sections populated.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 45: Attack Surface Analysis
practice_exercises.description: Identify and minimise the attack surface.
practice_exercises.expected_output: 1.INCREASES 2.DECREASES 3.INCREASES 4.DECREASES 5.INCREASES 6.DECREASES

hints.message: Attack surface = total entry points for attackers. More = bigger surface.
New endpoints, debug mode, third-party code = more surface. Removing/disabling/validating = less.
1.INCREASES 2.DECREASES 3.INCREASES 4.DECREASES 5.INCREASES 6.DECREASES

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 46: Security Requirements Traceability
practice_exercises.description: Trace requirements to implementations.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
SR-001: Passwords hashed
  Impl: bcrypt in UserService.register()
  Test: Unit test verifies stored value != input
SR-002: All API auth required
  Impl: AuthFilter on all /api/* endpoints
  Test: Integration test: unauthenticated request returns 401
SR-003: Admin actions audited
  Impl: AuditLogger.log() in AdminService
  Test: Verify log entry exists after admin action

hints.message: Each requirement maps to code (where) and test (how to verify).
Requirement -> specific class/method -> specific test case.
3 requirements each with implementation location and verification test.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 47: Business Impact Analysis
practice_exercises.description: Assess business impact of security failures.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Customer Data Breach:
  Financial: Fines (GDPR up to 4% revenue), lawsuits
  Reputation: Lost customer trust, media coverage
  Legal: Regulatory investigation, notification requirements
  Operational: Incident response, system rebuild
Payment Downtime:
  Financial: Lost sales, SLA penalties
  Reputation: Customer frustration
  Legal: PCI-DSS compliance issues
  Operational: Emergency response, manual processing

hints.message: Think about each type of impact for each failure scenario.
Data breach = fines + trust + lawsuits + IR. Downtime = lost revenue + SLA + frustration.
4 impact types per scenario: financial, reputation, legal, operational.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 48: Security Architecture Review
practice_exercises.description: Conduct a full architecture review.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
[PASS] Auth: Centralised authentication?
[PASS] Crypto: TLS 1.2+ for all connections?
[FAIL] Access: Complete mediation on all endpoints?
...
Score: 8/10

hints.message: Check each design principle and architecture pattern.
10 checks: auth, crypto, access, logging, input validation, error handling, sessions, secrets, SDLC, monitoring.
10 pass/fail checks across all security areas.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 49: Compliance Mapping
practice_exercises.description: Map security controls to compliance requirements.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Encryption at rest: GDPR Art.32, PCI-DSS Req.3, OWASP A02
Access control: GDPR Art.25, PCI-DSS Req.7, OWASP A01
Audit logging: GDPR Art.30, PCI-DSS Req.10, OWASP A09
Input validation: PCI-DSS Req.6, OWASP A03
Incident response: GDPR Art.33, PCI-DSS Req.12

hints.message: One control can satisfy multiple compliance requirements.
Encryption->GDPR+PCI+OWASP. Logging->GDPR+PCI+OWASP. Validation->PCI+OWASP.
Map 5 controls to their compliance requirements across 3 frameworks.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 50: Full Security Assessment
practice_exercises.description: Complete assessment of a system architecture.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
STRIDE: [6 threats identified]
Risk Scores: 2 CRITICAL, 2 HIGH, 2 MEDIUM
Principle Violations: 2 found (missing mediation, weak fail-safe)
Mitigations: [6 specific controls]
Rating: 7/10 - GOOD (needs mediation + fail-safe fixes)

hints.message: Combine STRIDE + risk assessment + design principles + mitigations.
Apply everything: STRIDE for threats, scores for priority, principles for design review, mitigations for fixes.
Full assessment: 6 STRIDE threats, 6 risk scores, 8 principle checks, 6 mitigations, overall rating.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 51: Build: STRIDE Analyser Tool
practice_exercises.description: Automated STRIDE analysis for system components.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
=== STRIDE: Login Form ===
[S] Credential theft -> MFA
[T] Request tampering -> HTTPS
[R] Deny login -> Audit log
[I] Password leak -> Hashing
[D] Brute force -> Rate limit
[E] Admin bypass -> RBAC

hints.message: Map each STRIDE category to common threats for the given component type.
For each letter, generate a relevant threat and mitigation based on the component.
Generic STRIDE template with component-specific threats and mitigations.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 52: Build: Risk Register
practice_exercises.description: Create a risk tracking system.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
=== RISK REGISTER ===
#1 SQL Injection     Score:20 CRITICAL  Status:MITIGATED
#2 Auth Bypass       Score:16 HIGH      Status:IN PROGRESS
#3 Data Leak         Score:12 HIGH      Status:OPEN
...

hints.message: List/Map of risk objects with score calculation and sorting.
Risk class with fields: name, L, I, score, mitigation, status. Sort by score desc.
Full register: add, score, sort, track status.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 53: Build: Design Review Checker
practice_exercises.description: Automated check against all design principles.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
=== DESIGN REVIEW ===
[PASS] Least Privilege
[FAIL] Fail-Safe Defaults -> Add default-deny to all access checks
...
Score: 6/8 GOOD

hints.message: Check each of the 8 Saltzer & Schroeder principles.
8 checks, each with pass/fail, failed ones get recommendations.
8 principle checks with recommendations for failures.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 54: Build: Security Gate System
practice_exercises.description: Implement SDLC security gates with pass/fail criteria.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Gate: Requirements->Design
  [PASS] Security requirements documented
  [PASS] Data classification complete
  RESULT: PROCEED

Gate: Testing->Deployment
  [PASS] Pen test complete
  [FAIL] Critical vulnerabilities unresolved
  RESULT: BLOCKED

hints.message: Each gate has criteria. ALL must pass to proceed.
Design gates with 2-3 criteria each. Any failure blocks the gate.
4 gates with criteria, some passing, some failing.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 55: Build: Threat Model Report Generator
practice_exercises.description: Auto-generate a complete threat model document.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
============================
THREAT MODEL: E-Commerce
============================
1. DESCRIPTION: Online shopping platform...
2. ASSETS: Customer PII, payment data...
3. ACTORS: External hackers, insiders...
4. BOUNDARIES: Internet->DMZ->App->DB
5. STRIDE: [6 threats]
6. MITIGATIONS: [6 controls]

hints.message: Template with sections filled based on app type.
Generate all 6 sections with content appropriate for the app type.
Full report generator with all sections populated.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 56: Build: Defence Layer Simulator
practice_exercises.description: Simulate attacks passing through multiple defence layers.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Attack: SQL Injection
  Layer 1 (WAF): PASS (obfuscated)
  Layer 2 (Validation): BLOCKED

Attack: XSS
  Layer 1 (WAF): BLOCKED

Attack: IDOR
  Layer 1 (WAF): PASS
  Layer 2 (Validation): PASS
  Layer 3 (Auth): PASS
  Layer 4 (Authz): BLOCKED

hints.message: Array of layer checks. Each attack passes through until caught.
Each layer is a function returning pass/block. Chain them together.
5 layers, 3+ attacks, show exactly where each is caught.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 57: Build: Access Control Policy Engine
practice_exercises.description: Implement a flexible policy-based access control system.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Policy: ADMIN + DELETE + business_hours -> ALLOW
Policy: USER + READ + any_time -> ALLOW
Policy: USER + DELETE + any_time -> DENY
Policy: ADMIN + DELETE + after_hours -> DENY

hints.message: Define policies as rules, evaluate each against the request.
Policy object with role, action, condition. Evaluate by matching best policy.
Policy engine with rule matching and condition evaluation.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 58: Build: Security Metrics Dashboard
practice_exercises.description: Track and display security KPIs.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
=== SECURITY DASHBOARD ===
Vulnerabilities: 0 Critical, 2 High, 5 Medium, 12 Low
Mean Time to Patch: 3.2 days (target: <7)
Security Test Pass Rate: 94% (target: >90%)
Incidents This Month: 1 (down from 3)
Compliance Score: 87/100 (PCI-DSS)

hints.message: Define KPIs, calculate values, format as dashboard.
5 metrics with values, targets, and trend indicators.
Dashboard with 5 KPIs: vulnerabilities, MTTP, test rate, incidents, compliance.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 59: Build: Complete SDLC Pipeline
practice_exercises.description: Implement security activities at every SDLC phase.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Phase 1: REQUIREMENTS
  Activity: Security requirements defined
  Gate: PASS
Phase 2: DESIGN
  Activity: STRIDE threat model complete
  Gate: PASS
Phase 3: IMPLEMENTATION
  Activity: Code review passed
  Gate: PASS
Phase 4: TESTING
  Activity: Pen test found 2 medium issues
  Gate: PASS (no critical/high)
Phase 5: DEPLOYMENT
  Activity: Hardening checklist complete
  Gate: PASS
Phase 6: MAINTENANCE
  Activity: Monitoring active
  Status: RUNNING

hints.message: Simulate each phase with activity + gate check.
6 phases, each with security activity and pass/fail gate.
Complete pipeline: 6 phases, 6 activities, 6 gates.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 60: Build: Full Security Architecture Document
practice_exercises.description: Generate a comprehensive security architecture.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
=== SECURITY ARCHITECTURE DOCUMENT ===
1. AUTH: Centralised OAuth2 + MFA
2. AUTHZ: RBAC with 4 roles
3. DATA: AES-256 at rest, TLS in transit
4. NETWORK: 4 zones (DMZ, App, DB, Mgmt)
5. LOGGING: Centralised SIEM
6. IR: Automated detection + response playbook
7. COMPLIANCE: GDPR + PCI-DSS mapped
8. RISKS: 5 risks tracked

hints.message: 8 sections covering all aspects of security architecture.
Each section covers one domain: auth, authz, data, network, logging, IR, compliance, risk.
Complete document with all 8 sections populated.
